Name: adaptive_latency_aware_inference
Title: Adaptive Latency-Aware Inference: A Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping Framework
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework can significantly reduce inference latency without compromising accuracy in deep learning models, addressing the need for efficient real-time AI applications.
Related Work: Existing works, such as KVTuner and Ladder-residual, focus on optimizing inference latency through mixed-precision and parallelism-aware architectures. However, they often overlook the potential of adaptive layer-skipping based on Jacobian evaluations, which can provide a novel approach to balancing latency and accuracy. Our proposal distinguishes itself by integrating a theoretical framework that leverages Jacobian dynamics for real-time inference optimization, rather than relying solely on predefined configurations or parallelism strategies.
Abstract: In this proposal, we introduce an innovative approach to optimizing inference latency in deep learning models using a Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework. Traditional methods for reducing inference time often involve mixed-precision quantization or model parallelism, which can introduce trade-offs in accuracy and not fully exploit the informational content of model layers. Our method dynamically evaluates the Jacobian of intermediate activations to determine the necessity of each layer during inference. By selectively skipping layers with minimal impact on output variance, we aim to significantly enhance inference speed while maintaining robust accuracy. We will conduct empirical evaluations across various model architectures and datasets, focusing on latency metrics, accuracy retention, and operational efficiency. This framework promises to deliver a novel pathway to achieving the throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier in latency-critical applications, setting a new benchmark in the field.
Experiments: 1. Implement the Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism in selected DNN architectures (e.g., ResNet, Transformers) and evaluate its performance against baseline models without layer skipping.
2. Measure inference latency and accuracy across various benchmarks, including CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and language processing tasks, to assess the effectiveness of the proposed method.
3. Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of different layer-skipping thresholds and configurations on both latency and accuracy.
4. Compare the results with existing state-of-the-art methods, such as KVTuner and Ladder-residual, to demonstrate the advantages of our approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the complexity of dynamically evaluating the Jacobian in real-time, which may introduce computational overhead. Additionally, the effectiveness of layer skipping may vary depending on the specific architecture and dataset characteristics. We will address these limitations through careful empirical validation and optimization of the Jacobian evaluation process.
